comments.text: The latter conjecture is also mentioned in page 234 of P. Ribenboim, My Numbers, My Friends: Popular Lectures in Number Theory, Springer, 2006. In this 2017 paper of Aktas and Murty the upper bound of $O(x^{2/5})$ is obtained for the problem. As far as I can tell this is still the best known bound.

In "Sentance, W. A. (1981). Occurrences of Consecutive Odd Powerful Numbers. The American Mathematical Monthly, 88(4), 272–274. https://doi.org/10.1080/00029890.1981.11995246", the author has proved that "there exists an infinite number of pairs of consecutive odd powerful numbers, for eg: (25,27), each pair being associated with a certain Pell equation". Also, in "https://www.fq.math.ca/Scanned/20-1/mcdaniel.pdf", W. L. McDaniel disproves a conjecture made by Golomb in [Go70] who conjectured that conjectured that "there are infinitely many integers that cannot be written as the difference of two powerful numbers.

The Sentance paper proves that, every pair of powerful $n-1,n+1$ is associated with the solution of a Pell equation. The latter is trivial: $n$ has to be even and $n^2-1$ is powerful itself, so by definition of the form $my^2$ where $m \mid y.$ Nevertheless, the difference 2 result is not related to the difference 1 question.
